title: Hawaiian Ho'oponopono and the Anger Cleared
dek: You have been told that forgiveness is a spiritual bypass, a way to gloss over the harm done to your body. But true Ho'oponopono is not a polite apology; it is a somatic clearing of the cords that bind your fascia to the person who hurt you. It is the work of untangling the masseter, releasing the psoas, and letting the biology of resentment finally metabolize.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Biology of the Unforgiven

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the first thing you notice is the tension in your jaw. The masseter muscle is locked, a tight band of unexpressed fury that has been holding on since the conversation three days ago. You were taught that anger is a moral failure, a sign that you are not evolved enough, not spiritual enough, not 'high-vibrational.' But your body knows the truth. This is not pathology; this is biology. The anger is a messenger, a somatic alarm bell ringing in the dark, demanding that you pay attention to the boundary that was crossed.

When we talk about forgiveness, especially in the context of Hawaiian Ho'oponopono, we often misunderstand it as a polite, sanitized apology. We think it means saying 'I forgive you' and moving on, while the body still holds the score. But the body does not care about your polite words. The body remembers the exact tone of voice, the slight shift in the room's temperature, the way your stomach dropped and your diaphragm seized. The body holds the resentment in the fascia, in the levator scapulae, in the tight knot just below your ribs.

Gabor Maté writes extensively about how our unexpressed emotions become biology. He notes that when we suppress our anger to maintain attachment, the body turns that anger inward. The immune system gets confused; the nervous system stays in a state of hyper-vigilance. You are not just 'holding a grudge'; your biology is actively defending you against a perceived threat. The anger is not a flaw in your character; it is a desperate attempt by your organism to keep you safe.

## The Somatic Reality of Ho'oponopono

Ho'oponopono is often translated as 'to make right,' but it is more accurately a process of realigning the energetic and somatic cords that connect us to others. It is not about letting the other person off the hook; it is about cutting the cord that is draining your own life force. When you hold onto anger, you are literally tethering your nervous system to the person who harmed you. Your psoas muscle remains contracted, ready to fight or flee, even when the person is miles away.

Resmaa Menakem teaches us that trauma and anger are not just psychological events; they are physiological events that live in the body. He speaks of the 'somatic abolitionism' required to heal, the need to settle the body and metabolize the historical and personal trauma stored in our tissues. Ho'oponopono, when practiced as a somatic clearing, is a form of this metabolization. It is the practice of saying, 'I see the cord between us, I feel the tension in my trapezius, and I choose to sever this connection for my own biological integrity.'

## The Four Phrases in the Flesh

The modern, simplified version of Ho'oponopono involves four phrases: 'I am sorry. Please forgive me. Thank you. I love you.' But to say these words only with the mind is to miss the medicine entirely. You must say them with the body.

When you say 'I am sorry,' you are not apologizing for being angry. You are apologizing to your own body for the times you ignored its signals, for the times you swallowed your fury to keep the peace. You feel the apology in your throat, in the vocal cords that were silenced.

When you say 'Please forgive me,' you are asking your own nervous system to forgive you for overriding its protective mechanisms. You are asking your diaphragm to soften, acknowledging that you forced it to stay rigid for so long.

When you say 'Thank you,' you are thanking the anger itself. You are thanking the masseter for locking down to prevent you from saying something that would have put you in danger. You are thanking the psoas for preparing you to run. You are acknowledging the profound intelligence of your animal body.

And when you say 'I love you,' you are directing that love to the bruised, furious, exhausted parts of your own biology. You are not necessarily saying it to the person who harmed you; you are saying it to the tissue, the fascia, the blood, and the bone that bore the brunt of the harm.

## The Herbal Allies for the Clearing

This work is heavy, and the body needs support to metabolize the released energy. We do not do this work unassisted. The plant world offers profound medicine for the nervous system and the liver, which in Traditional Chinese Medicine is the seat of anger.

[Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) is the great trophorestorative for the nervous system. When your nerves are frayed from holding onto resentment, when you feel brittle and easily triggered, Milky Oats rebuilds the myelin sheath. It is the somatic equivalent of a heavy, warm blanket over a shivering body.

[Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) is for the circular thinking, the 3:14 AM rumination where you replay the argument over and over. It settles the mind and allows the body to drop into parasympathetic rest. It is not a sedative that knocks you out; it is a boundary-maker that says, 'We are done thinking about this for tonight.'

And then there is [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20), the great mover of liver stagnation. In TCM, unexpressed anger stagnates the liver Qi, leading to tension in the neck and shoulders, headaches, and a feeling of being stuck. Dandelion root, roasted and bitter, moves the energy downward and outward, helping the body to literally digest and excrete the biochemical byproducts of fury.

## The Practice: Severing the Cord

Find a place to sit where your back is supported. Feel the weight of your pelvis on the chair or the floor. Notice the exact points of contact between your body and the ground.

Bring your attention to the person or situation that is the source of your anger. Notice immediately where your body constricts. Does your jaw tighten? Does your stomach clench? Does your breath become shallow? Do not try to change it. Just map the somatic response.

Visualize a thick, heavy cord connecting that area of constriction in your body to the person or situation. Notice the color, the texture, the weight of the cord.

Now, bring your attention to your hands. Feel the warmth and the power in your own hands. Imagine that your hands are holding a sharp, heavy blade—perhaps a traditional Hawaiian obsidian blade, or simply a pair of heavy iron shears.

Take a deep breath in, expanding your belly, and as you exhale forcefully through your mouth, visualize yourself cutting the cord. Feel the immediate snap and release of tension in your body. Watch the other end of the cord fall away from you.

Place your hands over the area of your body where the cord was attached. Apply firm, comforting pressure. Say to that part of your body: 'I am sorry. Please forgive me. Thank you. I love you.'

Repeat this as many times as necessary. The body learns through repetition.

## The Aftermath of the Clearing

When the anger clears, what is left is not necessarily a feeling of 'love and light.' What is left is often grief. Grief for the time lost, grief for the harm done, grief for the fact that the boundary had to be drawn in the first place. This, too, is biology. Grief is the metabolization of loss.

Allow the grief to move through you. It may feel like a heavy ache in the center of your chest, a physical weight pressing down on your sternum. Do not rush to fix it. Drink your Milky Oats infusion. Rest. Let the body do what it knows how to do.

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that 'the body keeps the score.' But the body also knows how to heal the score, if we give it the right conditions, the right support, and the permission to feel exactly what it is feeling.

Ho'oponopono is not a magic trick. It is a profound, somatic discipline. It is the daily work of checking in with your fascia, your muscles, your breath, and asking: 'Where am I still tethered? Where am I still holding the charge?' And then, with immense self-compassion and fierce biological loyalty, doing the work to cut the cord.

## FAQ

**Q: I tried Ho'oponopono, but I still feel angry. Did I do it wrong?**

**A:** No. Anger is not a switch you can just flip off. If the anger is still there, it means the body still perceives a threat or an injustice that has not been fully acknowledged. The practice is not to force the anger away, but to keep listening to it. Ask your body what it needs to feel safe. Sometimes the anger remains because the boundary is still being crossed.

**Q: Is it safe to do this practice if the person who harmed me is still in my life?**

**A:** Yes, but with caution. Ho'oponopono is an internal, somatic practice. You do not need to involve the other person. In fact, it is often safer and more effective to do it entirely on your own. The goal is to clear your own nervous system, not to change their behavior. If they are still actively harming you, the priority is physical and emotional safety, not forgiveness.

**Q: What if I don't feel anything when I say the four phrases?**

**A:** That is completely normal. Numbness is a biological defense mechanism. It is the body's way of protecting you from overwhelming sensation. Do not force feeling. Just notice the numbness. Say the phrases to the numbness itself. 'I see that you are numb. Thank you for protecting me.' Over time, as the body feels safer, the sensation will return.

**Q: Can I use other herbs besides the ones you mentioned?**

**A:** Absolutely. The plant world is vast. Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is excellent for the anxious, fluttering heart that often accompanies anger. Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) is a gentle but profound relaxant for the digestive tract, where so much tension is held. Trust your body's intuition when choosing plant allies.
